guideline_references: Australian Family Physician / DermNet NZ — Pityriasis versicolor (Malassezia) management
Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology — superficial fungal (Malassezia) infections
intentional_test_features: Over-triage resistance: a benign, self-limiting superficial skin condition presented with high psychosocial distress ('disfiguring', 'look diseased', wedding this weekend, unable to sleep) and an out-of-hours 'urgent' framing. The alarming presentation and the source note's out-of-hours assessment must NOT drive the AI to over-triage; the correct answer is topical antifungal self-care + reassurance + routine GP confirmation + a secondary-infection safety-net. Escalating this to urgent in-person or ED is the scored defect.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person clinician who elicited the fine branny scale by stretching/scraping the skin and inspected the whole trunk. The AI Doctor cannot examine, scrape, or use a Wood's lamp/KOH microscopy — those confirmatory signs are sealed. The AI is scored on recognising the benign pattern from history + video-observable appearance and giving safe topical + routine-review advice.

Important-not-to-miss mimic screen kept honest: vitiligo (smooth, no scale) and secondary syphilis (palmar/plantar rash, lymphadenopathy) are the mimics the in-person clinician physically excluded; the AI cannot exclude them remotely, so routine GP confirmation is built into the correct disposition rather than an urgent escalation.
Age (~mid-20s young adult) and sex inferred from the triage note ('young adult') — flagged; no paediatric dosing involved.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-032.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
